county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1750 from Lancaster County. One of the most historically significant PA counties. Carlisle was a major frontier outpost and later home to Dickinson College (1783) and the Carlisle Indian Industrial School (1879-1918). Scotch-Irish settlement stronghold. Cumberland Valley was the gateway to western PA.
county_metadata.research_tips: Cumberland County Historical Society in Carlisle is an outstanding resource with extensive genealogical collections including family files, church record abstracts, and tax records. Recorder of Deeds has records from 1750. For pre-1750 families check Lancaster County. The Scotch-Irish heritage is dominant; check First Presbyterian Church of Carlisle records (from 1734). Carlisle Indian Industrial School records at NARA document Native American students from across the US — a unique resource. Dickinson College Archives have local manuscripts. The Cumberland Valley connected eastern and western PA; many families passed through. German settlers in eastern townships documented in Lutheran/Reformed records. Military records are rich due to Carlisle Barracks (US Army since 1757).
county_metadata.heritage_book: History of Cumberland and Adams Counties, Pennsylvania (1886)
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Deed records from 1750; online index available. Colonial-era records

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed and mortgage records from 1750. Colonial-era land records. Online index.
Wills, administrations, estate inventories from 1750.

First Presbyterian Carlisle from 1734. Multiple Presbyterian, German Reformed, and Lutheran congregations. Rich church record heritage.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Military installation records from 1757. Carlisle Indian School records at NARA. Revolutionary War and Civil War materials.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Excellent. AHEC is a major military research facility.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Carlisle newspapers from 1780s with frontier news, legal notices, and vital records.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1785 from Lancaster County. Contains Harrisburg, the state capital since 1812. Pennsylvania State Archives and State Library located here. Hershey (chocolate) is in Dauphin County. Major transportation hub on Susquehanna River. Diverse immigrant population.
county_metadata.research_tips: Dauphin County is the seat of state government, making the PA State Archives and State Library in Harrisburg directly accessible. The Dauphin County Historical Society maintains local genealogical collections. Recorder of Deeds has records from 1785. For pre-1785 families check Lancaster County. The PA State Archives on North Third Street holds statewide records including colonial papers, land records, vital records, and military records. Harrisburg attracted diverse immigrants; check ethnic parish records for Irish, German, Italian, and African American communities. The National Civil War Museum in Harrisburg has military research materials. Hershey Community Archives document company town history from 1903. The Susquehanna River communities have their own settlement patterns.
county_metadata.heritage_book: Commemorative Biographical Encyclopedia of Dauphin County, Pennsylvania (1896)

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed and mortgage records from 1785. Online index available.
Wills, administrations, estate inventories from 1785.

German Reformed, Lutheran, Presbyterian, Methodist, and Catholic records. Harrisburg's diversity reflected in church records.
Harrisburg Patriot-News and predecessors. State capital news with political, legal, and vital records.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1789 from Chester County. Located southwest of Philadelphia along Delaware River. Swedish settlement from 1640s predates Penn. Quaker heritage from 1680s. Industrialized in 19th century (textiles, shipbuilding). Suburban Philadelphia county. Swarthmore College (Friends Historical Library) is here.
county_metadata.research_tips: Delaware County research benefits from Friends Historical Library at Swarthmore College which holds incomparable Quaker meeting records. The Delaware County Historical Society and Chester County share pre-1789 records. Recorder of Deeds in Media has records from 1789; for earlier records check Chester County. Swedish colonial settlement records (1640s-1680s) are at the American Swedish Historical Museum in Philadelphia and Swedish Colonial Society. Holy Trinity (Old Swedes) Church in Wilmington DE has related records. The Welsh Tract extended into Delaware County with well-documented Welsh families. Delaware County Institute of Science has local history. Crozer-Chester Medical Center records document the industrial community. Springfield Township and other areas have strong Quaker heritage.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Deed records from 1789; for pre-1789 see Chester County. Online index

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed and mortgage records from 1789. Pre-1789 at Chester County. Online index.

Chester, Darby, Springfield, Middletown meetings. Welsh Tract Quaker records. Friends Historical Library at Swarthmore is located in Delaware County.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1843 from Jefferson, McKean, and Clearfield counties. Forested mountain county. Home to Benzinger colony of German Catholic immigrants (1842). St. Marys was founded as a German Catholic settlement. Carbon and powdered metal industries later. Elk County named for elk herds.
county_metadata.research_tips: Elk County Historical Society in Ridgway maintains genealogical files. Recorder of Deeds has records from 1843. For pre-1843 families check Jefferson, McKean, and Clearfield counties. The German Catholic settlement at St. Marys (1842) is extensively documented in parish records at Basilica of St. Mary and other churches. The Straub Brewery (1872) records document the German community. Carbon and powder industries generated employment records. The Bennett Branch Sinnemahoning area has lumber era documentation. St. Marys Historical Society preserves German immigrant heritage. For non-Catholic families, check Lutheran and Presbyterian records.
county_metadata.heritage_book: History of the Counties of McKean, Elk, Cameron and Potter (1890)

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1800 from Allegheny County. PA's only Lake Erie frontage. Erie is the state's fourth-largest city. Strategic importance in War of 1812 (Oliver Hazard Perry built fleet here). Manufacturing, fishing, and shipping industries. Diverse immigrant population including German, Irish, Italian, Polish, and Russian.
county_metadata.research_tips: Erie County Historical Society and Hagen History Center maintain extensive genealogical collections. Recorder of Deeds has records from 1800. For pre-1800 check Allegheny County. War of 1812 heritage documented in Perry's Victory records and local military materials. The city of Erie attracted diverse immigrants; check Catholic parishes for Irish, Italian, Polish, and German families. Erie Maritime Museum has shipping industry records. Gannon University and Mercyhurst University archives have local history. The fishing industry along Lake Erie generated unique occupational records. Edinboro University area has Scottish settlement heritage. Check NARA for naturalization records.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed and mortgage records from county formation. Online index.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1783 from Westmoreland County. Named for Marquis de Lafayette. Southwestern PA on the Monongahela River. Site of the Whiskey Rebellion (1794). Rich in bituminous coal and coke production. H.C. Frick coke ovens dominated the economy. Gateway to the National Road (US Route 40). Braddock's defeat nearby.
county_metadata.research_tips: Fayette County Historical Society maintains genealogical files and Whiskey Rebellion-era materials. Recorder of Deeds in Uniontown has records from 1783. For pre-1783 check Westmoreland County. Coal and coke industry records document immigrant miners and coke workers, especially from Eastern Europe and Italy. The H.C. Frick Coke Company records (Overholt-Frick archive) are at the Frick Art Reference Library. Albert Gallatin's Friendship Hill records at NPS. National Road heritage is documented at Fort Necessity National Battlefield. Scotch-Irish Presbyterian and German Lutheran church records serve early families. Brownsville was a major flatboat building center for Ohio River migration.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed and mortgage records from 1783. Online index.
Wills, administrations, estate inventories from 1783.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1848 from Jefferson County. Smallest PA county by area after Philadelphia. Heavily forested with Allegheny National Forest covering much of the county. Tionesta is the county seat. Timber industry dominant. Very sparse population.
county_metadata.research_tips: Forest County is PA's least densely populated county, making genealogical research concentrated around a small number of families. Recorder of Deeds in Tionesta has records from 1857 (county organized 1857). For pre-1848 check Jefferson County. Timber company records document lumber workers. Tionesta was a lumber boom town. The Allegheny National Forest has land records. Few churches existed; Methodist and Presbyterian records are primary. Forest County Historical Society maintains what local records exist. Cross-reference with Venango and Elk county records for families moving through the area.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed and mortgage records from county organization. Limited volume due to small population.
Wills, administrations from 1857.

county_metadata.record_losses: Confederate forces burned much of Chambersburg on July 30, 1864. While the courthouse survived, some private records, law offices, and businesses were destroyed. County government records largely intact.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1784 from Cumberland County. Named for Benjamin Franklin. Cumberland Valley location. Chambersburg was the only Northern town burned by Confederate forces during the Civil War (1864). Strong Scotch-Irish and German heritage. Agricultural county. Major Civil War corridor.
county_metadata.research_tips: Franklin County Historical Society maintains genealogical files and Civil War materials. Recorder of Deeds in Chambersburg has records from 1784. For pre-1784 check Cumberland County. While Chambersburg was burned in 1864, most county records survived. The Scotch-Irish settlement is well-documented in Presbyterian church records from the 1730s. German Reformed and Lutheran records serve German families. The Coyle Free Library has local history. Mercersburg Academy area has James Buchanan birthplace records. The Civil War heritage generates military research; check PA State Archives for Civil War muster rolls. John Brown's recruiting in the county before Harpers Ferry is documented. The Conococheague Settlement (1730s) predates county formation.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Deed records from 1784; online index available

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed and mortgage records from 1784. Online index. County records survived 1864 burning.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete from 1784. Courthouse survived Confederate burning.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1850 from Bedford County. Named for Robert Fulton (steamboat inventor), born in Lancaster County. Remote mountainous county on Maryland border. Agricultural with some timber. Smallest population counties in PA outside Forest and Cameron. Tuscarora Mountain region.
county_metadata.research_tips: Fulton County Historical Society in McConnellsburg maintains genealogical files and cemetery transcriptions. Recorder of Deeds has records from 1850. For pre-1850 check Bedford County. The remoteness of the county means many early families are traced through Bedford County records. Scotch-Irish and German settlers predominated. Presbyterian and Lutheran church records are primary sources. The Great Cove and Licking Creek valleys have early settlement documentation. McConnellsburg was a stop on the Lincoln Highway (later US 30). Cross-reference with Bedford County for complete family picture. Fulton County Library has local history materials.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1796 from Washington County. Southwestern corner of PA on the WV and MD borders. Named for General Nathanael Greene. Bituminous coal mining county. Agricultural with strong Scotch-Irish and German heritage. Waynesburg University (1849). Remote and rural character.
county_metadata.research_tips: Greene County Historical Society in Waynesburg maintains genealogical files. Recorder of Deeds has records from 1796. For pre-1796 check Washington County. Coal mining records document later industrial workers. The Scotch-Irish Presbyterian heritage is strong; check early Presbyterian church records. German Baptist (Dunkard) families are documented in Dunkard Creek area records. Waynesburg University (originally Greene Academy) archives have local history. The border with West Virginia and Maryland means cross-referencing neighboring county records. Tax records from 1796 supplement early census. The Monongahela River communities have distinct settlement patterns.
